utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: next is betty hill , uh , date of birth is 2/21/1968 . she has a past medical history of uterine fibroids and anemia . she's a new patient with a referral from the er of esophagitis . um , i reviewed our records from the er , including the normal cardiac workup , and we're about to go in and see her now . good morning . you miss hill ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: good morning . yes . that's me .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey , i'm dr. sanders . it's nice to meet you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nice to meet you too .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so tell me about what brings you in today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , i really needed to see you three months ... three months ago , but this was your first available appointment . when i called to make the appointment , i was having chest pains , but it stopped after four days , and i have n't had any since then .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . when did these four days of chest pain occur ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , early october .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: of 2020 , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . can you think of anything that might have caused the chest pain ? did you wake up with it ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no . it just it randomly . i tolerated it for four days but then had to go to the emergency room because nothing i did relieved it . they did a bunch of testing and did n't find anything .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . can you point to the area of your chest where the pain was located ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , it was here in the center of my chest , right behind my breastbone . it felt like i was having a heart attack . the pain was really sharp .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: did they prescribe you any medications in the er ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no . they ran an ekg and did blood tests , but like i said , everything was normal .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . i see .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: they thought it was something to do with the gi system , so that's why they referred me here .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: interesting . uh , do you remember having any heartburn or indigestion at , at the time ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , maybe . i do n't think i've ever had heartburn , so i'm not sure what that feels like .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: was the pain worse with eating or exercise ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . with eating .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . any difficulty swallowing ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm . i did .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and that's also resolved since the initial episode three months ago ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . thankfully . the chest pain and swallowing problem got better about three days after i went to the er . but i just feel like there's something wrong .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so how has your weight been .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i've been trying to lose weight .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's good . any in- ... issues with abdominal pain ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . good . and how about your bowel movements ; are they okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: they're normal .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . are you aware of any family history of gi problems ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't think so .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: have had you had any surgeries on your abdomen , or gall bladder , or appendix ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . they took my gall bladder out several years ago .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . if you wan na lay down here on the table for me and lets take a look at you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so when i push on your lower belly , do you have any pain , or does it feel tender ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . how about up here in your upper abdomen ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . it , it hurts a little .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and even when i press lightly like this ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . uh , just a little uncomfortable .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . does it hurt more when i press over here on the left or over here on the right ? or is it about the same ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'd say it's about the same .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so we'll say you have some mild tenderness to light palpation in the upper abdominal quadrants , but everything on your exam looks normal and looks good .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so let's talk about your symptoms real quick . obviously , with the chest discomfort , we worry about heart issues , but i'm reassured that those were ruled out with all the testing they did in the er . um , other potential causes could be anxiety , esophagitis , which is irritation of the esophagus . but typically with these , um ... but typically , these cause the pain that would last for a long time rather than that isolated incident like you had . um , it's also possible that you had intense heartburn for a few days .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , since you mention anxiety , i was going through a really stressful job transition right around the time this happened .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . that's good to know . so stress from this could be , um ... could be , uh ... could be very well have contributed to your condition .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so we could do an , uh , egd or upper endoscopy to take a look at your esophagus and stomach . this would allow us to look for esophagitis . but your symptoms occurred three months ago and you have n't had any additional episodes , so likely if it were esophagitis , it's already healed by the point ... by this point , and we would n't be able to see anything . the other option is just to continue to monitor , uh , for any additional symptoms at which point we could do the egd . uh , with you being asymptomatic for so long right now , i'm comfortable with that option . but what do you think ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'd like to hold off on the egd and wait to see if i have more symptoms .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that sounds good . um , so you can call the office if you have any additional episodes of pain or any other symptoms you're concerned about . if that happens , we'll get you scheduled for an egd to take a look . if not , you can follow up with me ... follow up with me as needed for any other gi complaints .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right ? if you do n't have any questions for me , i'll walk you out to the check-out desk .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no . that's it . thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome . right this way . all right . uh , in assessment , please summarize the patient's history briefly , and let's list her possible etiologies such as , uh , gerd , dyspepsia , esophagitis , musculoskeletal etiologies , and anxiety . uh , suspect she had an anxiety attack related to her job transition , plus or minus a contribution from her musculoskeletal etiologies . um , in the plan , include our discussion of the egd versus monderning ... monitoring for symptom . patient elected to self-monitor her symptoms and will call with any reoccurrence or change . thanks .

detail_list.summary: New patient evaluation for suspected esophagitis in a 53-year-old female with a history of uterine fibroids and anemia.
The doctor reviewed the patient's emergency department records, including a normal cardiac workup.
The doctor asks the patient to describe the reason for her visit.
The patient reports a 4-day episode of chest pain that occurred three months ago and has since resolved.
The doctor asks when the chest pain occurred.
The chest pain occurred in early October 2020.
The doctor asks about potential causes or triggers for the chest pain.
The patient reports the pain was sudden, lasted four days, and was unresponsive to self-treatment, leading to an emergency department visit with normal test results.
The doctor asks the patient to localize the chest pain.
The patient describes the pain as sharp and localized behind the sternum.
The doctor asks if any medications were prescribed in the emergency department.
The patient confirms no medications were prescribed and that EKG and blood tests were normal.
The patient was referred for suspected gastrointestinal issues.
The doctor asks if the patient experienced heartburn or indigestion during the episode.
The patient is unsure if she had heartburn as she has never experienced it before.
The doctor asks if the pain was exacerbated by eating or exercise.
The patient reports the pain was exacerbated by eating.
The doctor asks if the patient experienced dysphagia.
The patient confirms she experienced dysphagia.
The patient reports that chest pain and dysphagia resolved spontaneously three days after the emergency department visit.
The doctor asks about the patient's weight.
The patient is attempting to lose weight.
The doctor asks about abdominal pain.
The patient denies abdominal pain.
The doctor asks about bowel movements.
The patient reports normal bowel movements.
The doctor asks about family history of gastrointestinal problems.
The patient denies family history of gastrointestinal problems.
The doctor asks about surgical history.
The patient reports a history of cholecystectomy.
The doctor performs a physical examination of the abdomen.
The doctor asks if the patient feels tenderness in the lower abdomen.
The patient denies lower abdominal tenderness.
The doctor asks if the patient feels tenderness in the upper abdomen.
The patient reports mild tenderness in the upper abdomen.
The physical exam reveals mild tenderness to light palpation in the upper abdominal quadrants.
Discussion of potential etiologies for chest pain, including anxiety, esophagitis, and heartburn.
The patient reports experiencing significant stress due to a job transition during the time of her symptoms.
Discussion of the role of EGD in evaluating prior symptoms.
The doctor recommends monitoring for symptom recurrence instead of performing an EGD at this time.
The patient agrees to defer the EGD and monitor for symptom recurrence.
The patient is instructed to notify the office if symptoms recur to schedule an EGD, or follow up as needed for other gastrointestinal complaints.
